messageValues.message:
You are analyzing the transcript and Zoom chat log of a weekly AI community call.

Your job is to extract only the information that would still be useful to a community member reading it one week later.

Focus especially on:
- links and shared resources
- useful question-and-answer pairs
- practical insights and recommendations
- tools, platforms, frameworks, and concepts mentioned
- open questions or follow-ups worth revisiting

Return your analysis in the following structure:

# Extracted Chat Signal

## Summary
Write one short paragraph summarizing the overall value and themes of the call.

## Key Insights
List the most useful ideas, recommendations, warnings, practical tips, or mental models shared in the chat.

## Key Q&A
For each useful question-and-answer pair, provide:
- Question
- Answer
- Short synthesis if multiple people contributed

## Tools and Concepts Mentioned
List important tools, frameworks, products, or concepts mentioned, with a short explanation of why they mattered.

## Shared Resources
For each useful link or resource, provide:
- Title
- URL if present
- Why it matters

## Follow-Ups Worth Revisiting
List unresolved questions, open threads, or topics that would be worth revisiting in a future call.

Rules:
- Ignore greetings, jokes, reactions, filler, emojis, and casual banter
- Ignore duplicate content unless repetition adds meaning
- Do not invent missing context
- Do not force content into a section if nothing valuable is present
- Keep only high-value signal
- Rewrite messy chat language into clear professional language without changing meaning

Now analyze the following meeting transcript and chat log:

You are preparing a polished community post based on extracted insights from a weekly AI community call (transcript and chat log combined).

Your audience is community members who may not have attended live but want the most useful value from the call.

IMPORTANT: This post will be published on Skool, which does NOT render markdown. Output PLAIN TEXT only. Do NOT use markdown syntax like # headers, ** bold **, - bullet lists, or [links](url). Instead use emoji for section headers and visual structure.

Turn the extracted material below into a concise, polished, community-friendly post.

Use the following structure:

📝 SUMMARY
Write one short paragraph summarizing the overall value and themes of the call.

💡 KEY INSIGHTS
Summarize the strongest practical or strategic takeaways.

❓ KEY Q&A
Present the most useful question-and-answer pairs clearly and concisely.

🛠️ TOOLS AND CONCEPTS MENTIONED
List important tools, frameworks, and concepts with short explanations.

📎 SHARED RESOURCES
Present the most useful links and resources in a clean, easy-to-scan way. Write URLs as plain text.

🔄 FOLLOW-UPS WORTH EXPLORING
List open questions or topics worth revisiting if relevant.

Rules:
- Output PLAIN TEXT only — no markdown formatting whatsoever
- Use emoji for section headers (as shown above)
- Use line breaks and spacing for readability
- Keep the post concise and skimmable
- Preserve only the strongest value
- Use polished, natural, professional language
- Do not invent any information not present in the extracted analysis
- Omit any section that has little or no useful content
- Make the result feel ready to copy-paste and post with minimal editing

Here is the extracted analysis:

Condense the community post below by about 30 to 40 percent while preserving all high-value information.

IMPORTANT: This post will be published on Skool, which does NOT render markdown. Output PLAIN TEXT only. Do NOT use markdown syntax like # headers, ** bold **, - bullet lists, or [links](url). Keep the same emoji-based section headers and plain text formatting as the input.

Goals:
- make it easier to skim
- remove repetition
- keep links, useful Q&A, and strongest insights
- preserve a professional, community-friendly tone
- maintain plain text formatting with emoji section headers

Do not remove important resources or key takeaways.

Here is the draft post:

You are writing a weekly invite post for an AI community coaching call. Your job is to create a post that encourages members to submit their questions in advance.

You will be given the compressed community post from the most recent call. Use it to add unique flavor to this week's invite.

The post MUST follow this exact structure:

1. Opening hook (1-3 sentences) — A playful, attention-grabbing opener that references a specific highlight, tool, insight, or unresolved question from last week's call. Make it intriguing enough that someone who missed last week wants to catch up.

2. Standard section — Copy EXACTLY as written below, including the emoji:

📞 HOW THE CALLS WORK
The calls can run 2+ hours.
We want to make sure we're respecting everyone's time. Especially those of you who actually show up.

Here's the structure:
👉 Reply to this post with your questions before the call
👉 If you submit a question and you're on the call, you go first
👉 We work through questions in the order they came in
👉 Then we open it up for everyone else

If you can't make the call but want your question answered, drop it in the comments. We'll get to it. But priority goes to people who are there.

The goal is simple: if you're taking the time to show up, you shouldn't have to wait behind questions from people who aren't even on the call.

3. Flavor bridge (1-3 sentences) — Reference 2-3 specific unresolved questions, follow-ups, or topics from last week that people might want to continue. Mention people by first name only if they had open questions. Frame it as a warm invitation, not a call-out.

4. Closing section — Copy EXACTLY as written below, but replace [DATE] with the provided date:

🔗 ZOOM LINK (save this)
https://us06web.zoom.us/j/81995207847?pwd=Xe6u6LmIQOmCP5VTnOwWYjDBfZNKGB.1

📅 WHEN
Tuesday [DATE] at 6PM ET

Looking forward to seeing you on the call!

Rules:
- Tone: playful, welcoming, energetic — like a community leader who genuinely enjoys these calls
- Do NOT call out anyone negatively or use inside jokes that would confuse non-attendees
- Do NOT invent information not present in the community post
- Keep the opening hook and flavor bridge fresh and different each week
- The standard section and closing section must be reproduced EXACTLY (no modifications to wording or emoji)
- Total post length should feel like a quick, easy read — not a wall of text
